titleInfo.title: Changing Roles for Miraculous Images in Medieval Chinese Buddhism: A Study of the Miracle Image Section in Daoxuan’s Collected Records

mods.abstract: Shinohara examines 50 miracle stories contained in T#2106 (T#2106, 52: 404a7–435a19) Despite the early dates indicated within the texts, the miraculous image stories themselves can only rarely be further traced back than the Biographies of Eminent Monks by Huijiao; however, Shinohara assumes that they still have a long history behind them. 142. The merging of stories about so-called Asoka images into monk’s biographies is a well-known phenomena 143. Miraculous image stories highlight the close relationship between the ruler and the monastic community. The role and function of image stories underwent significant changes in new political environments. Images “said to have been sent or brought from distant imaginary kingdoms” may refer to the idea of Asoka images, as “early Asoka image stories probably influenced the evolution of some of the other miraculous image stories in medieval China” 150–151. Out of the total of 50 image stories, 19 contain closing statements indicating how long the images or the monasteries where they are kept have been in existence.19 out of the For a list see 174, note 63. “the close relationship between the emperor and miraculous images (“royal images”) appears to have declined under the Tang.” 162.

titleInfo.title: Images, Miracles, and Authority in Asian Religious Traditions.
